topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The weight of the duvet felt like a physical anchor, pulling Leo down into a deeper kind of stillness. His eyelids were heavy curtains, each blink a deliberate, slow effort. A faint, rhythmic hum from the refrigerator was the loudest sound in the room. He shifted, a groan escaping his lips as his arm flopped over his face. Then, the phone buzzed on the bedside table. He dragged himself toward it, the effort making his limbs feel thick and unresponsive. The message read: "Party cancelled. So sorry." A slow, dull ache settled in his chest, heavy and flat. He stared at the screen, the letters blurring slightly at the edges. All the bright anticipation that had been buzzing under his skin just… deflated. He let his head drop back onto the pillow, the silence of the room pressing in, a profound, yielding quiet.

[story 2]

Elara was adrift in a warm, heavy haze. The light filtering through the blinds was a hazy, amber wash across the floor. Every movement required conscious negotiation; lifting a hand felt like moving through thick water. She managed to turn her head toward the notification on her tablet, her focus struggling to coalesce. The words swam before her eyes: "The celebration is off." A strange hollowness bloomed in her stomach, a sensation like a sudden drop in temperature. She didn't move to reply. Instead, she curled tighter into the sofa cushions, pulling the throw blanket up to her chin, seeking the enveloping comfort of inertia. Her breathing became shallow, measured, each inhale a slight surrender to the profound lassitude that claimed her.

[story 3]

Mark found himself sinking into the armchair like a stone dropped into deep water. The edges of his vision were softening, the room’s details blurring into soft, indistinct shapes. He blinked once, twice, trying to force his awareness back into sharp focus, but the effort felt monumental. The text message glowed on his phone, stark against the dim lighting: "Party postponed indefinitely." A slow, weary sigh escaped him, a sound that seemed too large for his tired frame. He didn't bother to read any further. His eyes drifted shut again, the desire to simply cease exerting any will overwhelming him. The warmth of the fabric against his skin was the only sensation registering as anything substantial.
